#  Introduction

Antiferroelectric (AFE) ceramics are distinguished by their antiparallel dipole arrangement, which yields a characteristic double polarization–electric field (P–E) loop and minimal remanent polarization. This unique structure makes them attractive for high-power capacitors, actuators, and energy-storage devices. However, conventional AFEs suffer from large hysteresis losses during the field-induced AFE-to-ferroelectric (FE) transition, limiting their energy storage efficiency. The growing demand for compact, high-performance pulsed-power systems has renewed interest in AFE ceramics, particularly in strategies to reduce hysteresis while maintaining high recoverable energy density (W_rec). Relaxor ferroelectrics (RFEs) and relaxor AFEs offer a promising alternative: their polar nanoregions (PNRs) and diffuse phase transitions inherently suppress hysteresis. Yet, achieving both ultrahigh W_rec and efficiency (η) simultaneously remains challenging. This work introduces heterogeneous dipolar structures (HDSs)—nanoscale mixtures of ordered and disordered AFE regions—as a new paradigm. By disrupting long-range AFE order through controlled Cd doping, the authors create local compositional and structural heterogeneity that delays polarization saturation and reduces hysteresis, as visualized in the phase-field simulations of Fig. 1. The atomic-scale evidence in Fig. 3 further confirms that HDSs consist of coexisting N=4 and N=5 modulation stripes with highly disordered displacement vectors, providing the structural basis for superior capacitive energy storage.
